title: AP World History: Modern DBQ Practice Exam 07

questions.prompt:
Evaluate the extent to which disease control encouraged new forms of state intervention and international cooperation from c. 1850 to 1950.
Document 1
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Statement by a French delegate at an international sanitary conference, 1851, Paris.
Source Type: Conference statement.
“Steamships and railways have reduced the time between distant ports and European cities. A disease that once traveled slowly with caravans can now arrive before authorities understand its origin. Quarantine rules must therefore be discussed among governments, not invented separately by each port in panic.”
Sourcing cues: Delegate from an industrializing European state; links disease control to transportation technology and international coordination.
Document 2
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Letter from an Ottoman quarantine officer to the Ministry of Health, 1866, Alexandria.
Source Type: Administrative letter.
“Pilgrims, merchants, and sailors arrive from many lands, and each group complains when detained. If inspection is too harsh, trade suffers and officials are accused of disrespecting religion. If inspection is too weak, disease may spread inland. We require trained doctors, clean stations, and rules accepted by foreign consuls as well as local authorities.”
Sourcing cues: Port health official in a major Mediterranean-Red Sea connection point; shows tension between trade, religion, diplomacy, and public health.
Document 3
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Description of a cholera-route map prepared for a public health journal, 1884, London.
Source Type: Described map.
The map uses arrows to show reported cholera outbreaks moving from South Asia through Red Sea ports, Mediterranean shipping lanes, and European railway connections. Major ports are marked with black circles, while inland rail junctions are marked with squares. A note below the map states: “Modern transportation makes local disease a matter of international concern.”
Sourcing cues: Medical-publication visual source; presents disease spread through transportation networks and encourages professional public health responses.
Document 4
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Report from a British colonial medical officer during a plague outbreak, 1897, Bombay.
Source Type: Colonial medical report.
“House inspections, hospital removals, and disinfection teams are necessary to stop contagion. Yet many residents hide the sick because they fear separation from family, religious offense, or loss of wages. Public health cannot succeed by police power alone, but delay allows the disease to spread through the city.”
Sourcing cues: Colonial medical official; emphasizes disease control but reveals local resistance to intrusive state measures.
Document 5
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Description of a public health poster issued by a municipal government, 1918, Buenos Aires.
Source Type: Described poster.
The poster shows a crowded tram, a coughing passenger, and a nurse pointing toward an open window. Below are instructions: “Avoid crowds when ill. Cover coughs. Report fever. Protect workers and families.” The poster includes symbols for factories, schools, and streetcars, suggesting that urban life requires public cooperation during influenza outbreaks.
Sourcing cues: Municipal public education poster; uses simple instructions to encourage mass participation in disease prevention.
Document 6
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Memorandum from the League of Nations Health Organization, 1925, Geneva.
Source Type: International organization memorandum.
“Reliable reporting of epidemics requires cooperation among ports, telegraph offices, laboratories, and ministries. Delayed information endangers neighboring states and trade partners alike. The Health Organization will collect data, circulate warnings, and encourage standard methods for vaccination, quarantine, and laboratory confirmation.”
Sourcing cues: International organization perspective; emphasizes technical expertise, communication, and standardized reporting.
Document 7
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Statement from a World Health Organization planning committee, 1948, Geneva.
Source Type: International planning statement.
“Health is no longer a matter of charity after disaster. It is a responsibility of governments and a concern of the international community. Epidemic control, vaccination, nutrition, sanitation, and medical training must be coordinated so that poorer countries are not left as reservoirs of preventable disease.”
Sourcing cues: Post-World War II international health perspective; frames health as a government responsibility and global development issue.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 7
Thesis / Claim — 1 point: Earns the point by making a historically defensible claim that evaluates the extent to which disease control encouraged new state intervention and international cooperation from c. 1850 to 1950. A strong thesis should establish a line of reasoning, such as arguing that faster transportation and epidemic threats expanded government regulation, public education, medical bureaucracy, and international health institutions, while also creating conflicts over trade, religion, colonial power, and personal liberty.
Contextualization — 1 point: Earns the point by describing a broader historical context relevant to disease control, such as industrialization, steamship travel, railway expansion, urbanization, imperialism, migration, pilgrimage routes, public health science, germ theory, World War I, influenza, the League of Nations, World War II, or the creation of new international organizations.
Evidence from the Documents — 2 points: Earns 1 point by accurately using the content of at least three documents to address the prompt. Earns 2 points by using at least six documents to support an argument about quarantine, transportation, state health intervention, colonial medicine, public education, epidemic reporting, laboratories, vaccination, or international health institutions.
Evidence Beyond the Documents — 1 point: Earns the point by using at least one specific piece of relevant outside evidence not found in the documents, such as germ theory, Louis Pasteur, Robert Koch, the Suez Canal, the 1918 influenza pandemic, smallpox vaccination campaigns, urban sanitation reforms, colonial plague measures in India, the League of Nations, or the creation of the World Health Organization.
Sourcing / Analysis of Documents — 1 point: Earns the point by explaining how or why the point of view, purpose, audience, or historical situation of at least three documents is relevant to the argument. Effective examples include explaining why port officials balanced trade and quarantine, why colonial medical officers emphasized police power and resistance, why posters used simple public instructions, why international organizations focused on reporting standards, or why postwar planners framed health as a global responsibility.
Complexity — 1 point: Earns the point by demonstrating a more complex understanding of disease control. This may include explaining that public health expanded state power while also requiring popular cooperation, that international cooperation protected both lives and commerce, that colonial medicine could combine health goals with coercive authority, or that disease control shifted from emergency quarantine toward broader ideas of development, sanitation, vaccination, and medical training.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong DBQ could argue that disease control from c. 1850 to 1950 encouraged states to intervene more directly in travel, cities, homes, hospitals, reporting systems, and public education, while also encouraging international cooperation through conferences and health organizations. Documents 1, 2, 3, and 6 support arguments about transportation, quarantine, and international reporting. Documents 4 and 5 support arguments about state intervention and public cooperation or resistance. Document 7 shows the broader postwar shift toward health as a global responsibility. Outside evidence could include germ theory, Pasteur, Koch, the Suez Canal, the 1918 influenza pandemic, smallpox vaccination, urban sanitation, or the WHO. Strong sourcing should compare the priorities of diplomats, port officers, colonial doctors, municipal governments, and international health planners.
